## Timing {#sec-timing-incentives}

### Incentives

For type $t \in \{\ell, H\}$, define the gain from moving second rather
than first as

$$
R_t \equiv U_L^{ML}(V_t) - U_L^{S}(V_t).
$$ {#eq-rent-type}

Thus $R_t > 0$ means type $t$ prefers to move second, while
$R_t < 0$ means type $t$ prefers to move first.

Writing $u = \sqrt{V_t}$ and $c \equiv V\kappa/2$, we can rewrite the
rent as

$$
R(u)
=
\left(c-u-\frac{u^2}{2\sqrt{V}}\right)
\left(c-u+\frac{u^2}{2\sqrt{V}}\right).
$$ {#eq-rent-function}

$$
R(u) > 0
\iff
c < u - \frac{u^2}{2\sqrt{V}}
\quad \text{or} \quad
c > u + \frac{u^2}{2\sqrt{V}}.
$$ {#eq-rent-positive}

Equivalently, type $t$ prefers to move first if and only if

$$
u_t - \frac{u_t^2}{2\sqrt{V}}
\leq
\frac{V\kappa}{2}
\leq
u_t + \frac{u_t^2}{2\sqrt{V}}.
$$ {#eq-first-move-interval}

::: {#prp-exact-condition}
#### Exact Type-by-Type Condition

Let $c \equiv V\kappa/2$. Then type $t$ prefers to move first if and
only if $c$ lies in the interval

$$
I_t
\equiv
\left[
u_t - \frac{u_t^2}{2\sqrt{V}},
\;
u_t + \frac{u_t^2}{2\sqrt{V}}
\right].
$$

Equivalently, type $t$ prefers to move second whenever $c$ lies
strictly outside $I_t$.
:::

::: {.proof}
This is just the factorisation in @eq-rent-function. The rent is
non-positive precisely when the two factors have opposite signs, which
is equivalent to @eq-first-move-interval.
:::

::: {#prp-low-more-wait}
#### Low Type Has the Stronger Incentive to Wait

The low type always has at least as much second-mover rent as the high
type:

$$
R_\ell \geq R_H.
$$

with strict inequality whenever $\delta > 0$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Let $a = u_\ell$ and $b = u_H$, so $0 < a < b$ and
$\kappa = \frac{1}{2a} + \frac{1}{2b}$. Direct algebra gives

$$
R_\ell - R_H
=
\frac{(b^2-a^2)\left[2(V-ab)^2 + ab(b-a)^2\right]}{4Vab}.
$$ {#eq-rent-difference}

Every factor on the right-hand side is non-negative, and the first
factor is strictly positive when $\delta > 0$. Hence
$R_\ell > R_H$ for any non-degenerate binary distribution.
:::

::: {#cor-three-profiles}
#### Only Three Timing Profiles Are Possible

For Labor's two types, the only possible timing-preference profiles are:

1. both types prefer moving first: $R_\ell \leq 0$;
2. only the low type prefers moving second: $R_\ell > 0 \geq R_H$;
3. both types prefer moving second: $R_H > 0$.

The reverse pattern, in which only the high type prefers moving
second, is impossible.
:::

::: {#rem-mean-range}
#### Mean and Range Jointly Matter

The comparison between $\mu$ and $V$ is informative but not sufficient
by itself. The object that enters timing incentives is
$c = V\kappa/2$, and $\kappa$ depends on both the mean and the range of
the binary distribution. As a result, $\mu < V$ does not by itself rule
out second-mover incentives, and $\mu > V$ does not by itself guarantee
them. The exact criterion is the interval test in @prp-exact-condition.
:::

::: {#rem-delta-range}
#### Role of the Range Parameter $\delta$

For fixed $\mu$, increasing $\delta$ raises $\kappa$ and hence raises
$c = V\kappa/2$. At the same time it spreads the type-specific
intervals $I_\ell$ and $I_H$ farther apart. This is the sense in which
the range parameter controls whether the informed player wants to move
first or second. In the sandbox verification below, wider ranges
typically shift the economy from "both wait" to "only the low type
waits" and eventually to "both move first".
:::
